macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishnah on Images and the Problem of Permitting/Forbidding

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and review of the Mishnah on items with sun, moon, and dragon images
Statement of Rav Sheshet: which constellations, faces, and figures are permitted or forbidden
Introduction to the three-way distinction: making, worshipping, and finding idolatrous objects

macro_segments.title: The Dragon Figure: Definition and Identification

micro_segments.title: Baraita on what constitutes a dragon shape: any creature with spikes between its joints
Rabba bar bar Chana's story of finding a ring with a dragon image and nullifying its status
Three lessons learned from the ring story: a non-owner can nullify, knowledge is required, and coercion is permitted

macro_segments.title: The Problem of Ownership and Yeush: Lost Objects and Abandonment of Status

micro_segments.title: The issue of whether an owner abandons hope of recovering an idolatrous object found in a public place
Abaye's distinction: yeush applies to ownership but not to the idolatrous status itself
Explanation of why the object can be worshipped by another idolater or resold to one by a Jew

macro_segments.title: Rabban Gamliel's Moon Charts: Reconciling the Prohibition on Making Images with Educational Use

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah's account of Rabban Gamliel having moon shape images in his attic to teach others
The Biblical prohibition on making images of God's attendants from Exodus 20
Discussion of whether the prohibition applies to celestial servants on high versus earthly servants below

macro_segments.title: Abaye's Proposals for Allowing Images: Upper vs. Lower Servants and the Four-Faced Chariot

micro_segments.title: Abaye's first attempt: the prohibition applies only to lower celestial servants, not upper ones
Challenge: the four-faced figures of the heavenly chariot are forbidden, but this would permit human faces and sun/moon
Teaching of Rabbi Yehuda: the prohibition is against making representations of God Himself, not other servants
Counter-challenge: the Decalogue explicitly forbids figures in heaven and on earth, including all servants

macro_segments.title: The Critical Distinction: Making vs. Worshipping; Educational vs. Idolatrous Use

micro_segments.title: The baraita refers to worship, not making: the prohibition is against worshipping celestial figures, not forming them
Even the tiniest creature cannot be worshipped; the prohibition extends to all created things
Question: if making is allowed, who permits Rabban Gamliel to own these moon images?

macro_segments.title: Solutions for Ownership and Display: Others Making It, Public Context, and Educational Purpose

micro_segments.title: First solution: others made the images for Rabban Gamliel, so he did not violate the prohibition against making
Parallel case of Rav Yehuda and Shmuel: disfiguring an idol to reduce its sanctity, and reference to a destroyed synagogue
The principle of public vs. private: many people visit Rabban Gamliel, making the context quasi-public
Alternative: the moon images were incomplete or fragmented; complete figures are forbidden
Final solution: educational use exempts the prohibition—one may learn to understand and teach, even if not to make

macro_segments.title: Classification of Idolatrous Objects by Status: Honorable vs. Disgraceful Items

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon ben Gamliel's distinction: honorable idolatrous items are forbidden; disgraceful ones are permitted
Rav's interpretation: location determines status—items above water are honorable; items below water are disgraceful
Shmuel's interpretation: both above and below can be honorable or disgraceful depending on type of vessel
Baraita listing honorable items (bracelets, nose rings, rings) and disgraceful items (pots, kettles, linens)

macro_segments.title: Destruction of Idols and the Problem of Benefit: Rabbi Yose's Method and the Golden Calf Parallel

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yose: one may grind an idol and scatter it to the wind, or cast it to the sea
Problem: grinding the idol may mix it with manure, indirectly benefiting a Jew from an idol
The verse 'nothing shall cling to you from the condemned thing' addresses this concern spiritually
Reference to the Golden Calf story and its connection to idol inquiry and guilt determination

topical_tags.term: Nullification of idol status (Bitul Avodah Zarah)
Honorable vs. Disgraceful objects (Mechubad vs. Mevuzeh)
